Guideline: Use multi-stage builds with distroless final image
Description: Separate build and runtime stages to minimize image size, attack surface, and build cache efficiency
Do: Use a builder stage with golang image and copy only the binary to a distroless or scratch final stage
Dont: Ship the full Go toolchain and source code in the production image
Code Good:
# syntax=docker/dockerfile:1
FROM golang:1.22 AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY go.mod go.sum ./
RUN go mod download
COPY . .
RUN CGO_ENABLED=0 GOOS=linux go build -ldflags="-s -w" -o /server ./cmd/server

FROM gcr.io/distroless/static:nonroot
COPY --from=builder /server /server
USER nonroot:nonroot
ENTRYPOINT ["/server"]
Code Bad:
FROM golang:1.22
WORKDIR /app
COPY . .
RUN go build -o /server ./cmd/server
ENTRYPOINT ["/server"]

Guideline: Use gcr.io/distroless/static for Go binaries
Description: Distroless images contain only the application and its runtime dependencies, no shell, no package manager, reducing CVE exposure
Do: Use gcr.io/distroless/static for statically linked Go binaries or gcr.io/distroless/base for CGO-enabled binaries
Dont: Use alpine or ubuntu as base images for production Go services that do not need a shell
Code Good:
FROM gcr.io/distroless/static:nonroot
COPY --from=builder /app/server /server
USER nonroot:nonroot
ENTRYPOINT ["/server"]
Code Bad:
FROM ubuntu:22.04
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y ca-certificates
COPY server /server
ENTRYPOINT ["/server"]

Guideline: Use CGO_ENABLED=0 and linker flags for production builds
Description: Disable CGO for fully static binaries, strip debug info with ldflags to reduce binary size
Do: Set CGO_ENABLED=0 and use -ldflags with -s -w flags for production builds
Dont: Build with default CGO enabled and debug symbols in production containers
Code Good:
RUN CGO_ENABLED=0 GOOS=linux GOARCH=amd64 go build \
  -ldflags="-s -w -X main.version=$(git describe --tags)" \
  -trimpath \
  -o /app/server ./cmd/server
Code Bad: RUN go build -o /app/server ./cmd/server

Guideline: Implement /healthz and /readyz HTTP endpoints
Description: Kubernetes uses liveness and readiness probes to manage pod lifecycle; /healthz checks process health, /readyz checks if ready to serve traffic
Do: Implement separate liveness and readiness endpoints that check relevant dependencies
Dont: Use a single health endpoint for both liveness and readiness or omit probes entirely
Code Bad:
// No health endpoints defined
http.ListenAndServe(":8080", handler)

Guideline: Check dependent services in readiness probe
Description: Readiness probes should verify that all dependencies (database, cache, message broker) are reachable before accepting traffic
Do: Check connectivity to all critical dependencies in the readiness handler
Dont: Return 200 from readiness without verifying downstream dependencies

Guideline: Handle SIGTERM for graceful shutdown in Kubernetes
Description: When Kubernetes sends SIGTERM, the application should stop accepting new requests, finish in-flight requests, close connections, and exit cleanly within the terminationGracePeriodSeconds window
Do: Use signal.NotifyContext or signal.Notify to catch SIGTERM and shut down HTTP server gracefully
Dont: Call os.Exit or log.Fatal immediately on shutdown signal without draining connections

Guideline: Mount configuration via ConfigMap volumes or environment variables
Description: Use Kubernetes ConfigMaps to externalize configuration from the container image, allowing the same image across environments
Do: Mount ConfigMaps as files or inject as environment variables and read them at startup
Dont: Hardcode configuration values or bake environment-specific config into the container image

Guideline: Mount secrets from Kubernetes Secret volumes
Description: Use Kubernetes Secrets for sensitive data like database passwords, API keys, and TLS certificates, never bake them into images or ConfigMaps
Do: Mount secrets as files or environment variables from Kubernetes Secret objects
Dont: Hardcode secrets in code or store them in ConfigMaps or container images
Code Bad:
// Hardcoded secret in source code
dsn := "postgres://app:supersecret@db:5432/mydb"

Guideline: Set CPU and memory resource requests and limits
Description: Define resource requests for scheduling and limits for protection against runaway processes in Kubernetes pod specs
Do: Set both requests and limits for CPU and memory based on load testing benchmarks
Dont: Deploy pods without resource requests or limits allowing unbounded resource consumption
Code Good:
# deployment.yaml
resources:
  requests:
    cpu: "100m"
    memory: "128Mi"
  limits:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "512Mi"
Code Bad:
# deployment.yaml
# No resource limits defined
containers:
  - name: app
    image: myapp:latest

Guideline: Configure HPA for automatic scaling based on metrics
Description: Use Kubernetes HPA to automatically scale the number of pod replicas based on CPU utilization, memory usage, or custom metrics
Do: Define HPA with appropriate min/max replicas and target utilization based on observed performance
Dont: Manually scale deployments or set fixed replica counts without autoscaling
Code Good:
# hpa.yaml
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: go-service
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: go-service
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 10
  metrics:
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: cpu
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 70
Code Bad:
# Fixed replicas, no autoscaling
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
spec:
  replicas: 5

Guideline: Use controller-runtime for building Kubernetes operators
Description: The controller-runtime library provides abstractions for building Kubernetes operators including managers, controllers, and reconcilers
Do: Use controller-runtime manager and controller patterns to watch and reconcile custom resources
Dont: Write raw client-go informer and workqueue code when controller-runtime handles the boilerplate

Guideline: Define CRDs using kubebuilder markers
Description: Use kubebuilder markers to generate CRD manifests, deepcopy methods, and RBAC rules from Go type definitions
Do: Define API types with kubebuilder validation markers and generate manifests with controller-gen
Dont: Write CRD YAML manifests by hand and maintain them separately from Go types
Code Bad:
// Manually written CRD YAML, not synced with Go types
apiVersion: apiextensions.k8s.io/v1
kind: CustomResourceDefinition
metadata:
  name: myapps.example.com
# Manually maintained, drifts from code...

Guideline: Implement idempotent Reconcile functions
Description: The reconciler should compare desired state with actual state and make changes to converge, returning requeue if needed
Do: Make Reconcile idempotent and return ctrl.Result with appropriate requeue behavior
Dont: Perform non-idempotent operations or ignore errors in the reconcile loop

Guideline: Enable leader election for singleton operators
Description: When running multiple replicas of an operator, leader election ensures only one instance actively reconciles to prevent conflicts
Do: Enable leader election in the controller-runtime manager options
Dont: Run multiple operator replicas without leader election causing duplicate reconciliations

Guideline: Structure Helm charts for Go microservices
Description: Use Helm to template Kubernetes manifests for Go services, enabling consistent deployments across environments with configurable values
Do: Create a well-structured Helm chart with configurable values for image tag and resource limits and probes
Dont: Copy-paste raw YAML manifests and manually edit values for each environment
Code Good:
# values.yaml
replicaCount: 2
image:
  repository: myregistry/go-service
  tag: v1.0.0
  pullPolicy: IfNotPresent
resources:
  requests:
    cpu: 100m
    memory: 128Mi
  limits:
    cpu: 500m
    memory: 512Mi
probes:
  liveness:
    path: /healthz
  readiness:
    path: /readyz
Code Bad:
# dev-deployment.yaml (copy-pasted for each env)
image: myregistry/go-service:v1.0.0
# staging-deployment.yaml (another copy)
image: myregistry/go-service:v1.0.0
# Values manually changed in each file

Guideline: Expose /metrics endpoint with prometheus/client_golang
Description: Use the Prometheus client library to expose application metrics at /metrics for scraping by Prometheus, including custom business metrics
Do: Register custom metrics and expose them via promhttp.Handler on a separate port or path
Dont: Log metrics to stdout or implement custom metrics formats instead of using the Prometheus standard

Guideline: Instrument services with OpenTelemetry and OTLP exporter
Description: Use OpenTelemetry SDK to produce distributed traces and export them via OTLP to backends like Jaeger, Tempo, or Datadog
Do: Initialize a TracerProvider with OTLP exporter and propagate trace context across service boundaries
Dont: Use ad-hoc logging for tracing or implement vendor-specific tracing libraries directly
Code Good:
import (
    "go.opentelemetry.io/otel"
    "go.opentelemetry.io/otel/exporters/otlp/otlptrace/otlptracegrpc"
    "go.opentelemetry.io/otel/sdk/trace"
    "go.opentelemetry.io/otel/sdk/resource"
    semconv "go.opentelemetry.io/otel/semconv/v1.21.0"
)

exporter, err := otlptracegrpc.New(ctx, otlptracegrpc.WithEndpoint("otel-collector:4317"), otlptracegrpc.WithInsecure())
if err != nil {
    log.Fatalf("creating exporter: %v", err)
}
tp := trace.NewTracerProvider(
    trace.WithBatcher(exporter),
    trace.WithResource(resource.NewWithAttributes(semconv.SchemaURL, semconv.ServiceName("my-service"))),
)
otel.SetTracerProvider(tp)
defer tp.Shutdown(ctx)

Guideline: Use structured JSON logging for Kubernetes environments
Description: Structured JSON logs enable efficient parsing by log aggregators like Fluentd, Loki, and Elasticsearch in Kubernetes clusters
Do: Use log/slog or zerolog for structured JSON logging with consistent field names
Dont: Use fmt.Println or log.Printf with unstructured text messages in containerized services
Code Good:
import "log/slog"

logger := slog.New(slog.NewJSONHandler(os.Stdout, &slog.HandlerOptions{
    Level: slog.LevelInfo,
}))
slog.SetDefault(logger)

slog.Info("request handled",
    slog.String("method", r.Method),
    slog.String("path", r.URL.Path),
    slog.Int("status", statusCode),
    slog.Duration("duration", elapsed),
    slog.String("trace_id", traceID),
)
Code Bad:
log.Printf("handled %s %s in %v with status %d", r.Method, r.URL.Path, elapsed, statusCode)
// Unstructured text, hard to parse and filter in log aggregators

Guideline: Read configuration from environment variables
Description: Follow the twelve-factor app methodology by reading configuration from environment variables, with sensible defaults for development
Do: Use os.Getenv with fallback defaults or a library like caarlos0/env for typed config parsing
Dont: Hardcode configuration values or read from files only without environment variable override

Guideline: Use init containers for database migrations and setup tasks
Description: Run database migrations, schema validation, or dependency checks in init containers before the main application starts
Do: Run migrations in an init container using a migration tool like golang-migrate
Dont: Run migrations on application startup which can cause race conditions with multiple replicas
Code Good:
# deployment.yaml
initContainers:
  - name: migrate
    image: migrate/migrate:v4
    command:
      - migrate
      - -path=/migrations
      - -database=$(DATABASE_URL)
      - up
    envFrom:
      - secretRef:
          name: db-credentials
    volumeMounts:
      - name: migrations
        mountPath: /migrations

Guideline: Use sidecar containers for cross-cutting concerns
Description: Deploy sidecar containers alongside the main Go application for logging agents, proxy, or monitoring collectors without modifying app code
Do: Use sidecar containers for concerns like log shipping or TLS termination keeping the main container focused
Dont: Bundle all cross-cutting concerns into the main application container increasing complexity and coupling
Code Good:
# deployment.yaml with logging sidecar
containers:
  - name: app
    image: myapp:v1.0.0
    ports:
      - containerPort: 8080
    volumeMounts:
      - name: logs
        mountPath: /var/log/app
  - name: log-shipper
    image: fluent/fluent-bit:latest
    volumeMounts:
      - name: logs
        mountPath: /var/log/app
        readOnly: true

Guideline: Design Go services for service mesh compatibility
Description: Build Go services that work well with service mesh proxies like Istio or Linkerd by using proper HTTP semantics and health endpoints
Do: Use standard HTTP/gRPC protocols and propagate trace headers for mesh observability
Dont: Implement custom service discovery or mTLS in application code that conflicts with the mesh

Guideline: Define PodDisruptionBudget for availability during updates
Description: A PDB ensures that a minimum number of pods remain available during voluntary disruptions like node drains, cluster upgrades, and rolling deployments
Do: Create a PDB with minAvailable or maxUnavailable to protect service availability during planned disruptions
Dont: Deploy without PDB allowing all pods to be evicted simultaneously during cluster operations
Code Good:
# pdb.yaml
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: go-service-pdb
spec:
  minAvailable: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: go-service
---
# Or use maxUnavailable
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: go-service-pdb
spec:
  maxUnavailable: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: go-service
Code Bad:
# No PDB defined
# During node drain, all pods can be evicted at once
# kubectl drain node-1 --ignore-daemonsets
# All go-service pods terminated simultaneously

Guideline: Restrict pod-to-pod communication with NetworkPolicy
Description: Use Kubernetes NetworkPolicy resources to implement microsegmentation, allowing only expected traffic between pods
Do: Define ingress and egress network policies to restrict traffic to only known communication paths
Dont: Run pods with default allow-all network policy permitting unrestricted lateral movement
Code Good:
# networkpolicy.yaml
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: go-service-netpol
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: go-service
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
    - Egress
  ingress:
    - from:
        - podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: api-gateway
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 8080
  egress:
    - to:
        - podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: postgres
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 5432
Code Bad:
# No network policies
# Any pod in the namespace can communicate with any other pod
# Compromised pod has unrestricted network access

Guideline: Run govulncheck and trivy in CI pipelines
Description: Integrate vulnerability scanning into CI/CD pipelines to catch known vulnerabilities in Go dependencies and container images before deployment
Do: Run govulncheck for Go-specific vulnerabilities and trivy for container image CVEs in every CI build
Dont: Skip vulnerability scanning or run it only manually on an ad-hoc basis
Code Good:
# CI pipeline step
steps:
  - name: Go vulnerability check
    run: |
      go install golang.org/dl/govulncheck@latest
      govulncheck ./...

  - name: Container image scan
    run: |
      docker build -t myapp:${{ github.sha }} .
      trivy image --exit-code 1 --severity HIGH,CRITICAL myapp:${{ github.sha }}
Code Bad:
# No scanning in CI
steps:
  - name: Build and push
    run: |
      docker build -t myapp:latest .
      docker push myapp:latest
      # No vulnerability scanning, ship and hope
